insight: At the first hydration break, Iraq's attacking momentum remained relatively stagnant, with their actions per minute increasing by only 0.10, going from 0 to 1 action, indicating a slow start to the game. In contrast, Norway's attacking momentum showed a slight increase, with their actions per minute also increasing by 0.10, but from a higher base of 3 to 4 actions, suggesting they were able to maintain a moderate level of pressure on Iraq. Overall, Norway benefited more from the early hydration break, as they were able to build on their existing attacking momentum, whereas Iraq struggled to gain traction. This subtle difference in momentum could have a significant impact on the remainder of the match, as Norway's continued pressure may force Iraq to defend deeply and limit their own scoring opportunities.
